statement: Prime Minister Kristrún Frostadóttir (Samfylkingin) stated in Alþingi on 9 March 2026 that she has been clear in all conversations, including with EU leaders, that any Icelandic accession process would need to respect fundamental principles about Iceland's way of life as an island nation, particularly regarding energy and fisheries resources. She described these as 'grundvallarprinsipp sem þarf að virða' (fundamental principles that must be respected) and said she would set 'skýr rauð flögg' (clear red flags) on these issues in any negotiations. However, she declined to specify exact negotiating terms in parliament, saying: 'Nákvæmu orðalagi í þessari pontu ætla ég ekki að stilla upp' (I will not lay out precise wording at this podium). She also clarified that the referendum decision predates the current economic situation and was part of the coalition agreement, not an economic rescue measure.
caveats: The PM's statements on resource sovereignty are general commitments without specific detail on negotiating red lines. The refusal to specify exact terms in parliament could reflect either strategic negotiating prudence or a lack of defined positions. The PM's stance that EU accession is a long-term strategic decision, not an economic quick fix, contrasts with some media framing.

related_entries.statement: The coalition government formed in late 2024, comprising Samfylkingin (Social Democrats, 13 seats), Viðreisn (Reform Party, 11 seats), and Flokkur fólksins (People's Party, 8 seats), agreed in their coalition agreement to hold a referendum on whether to resume EU accession negotiations by 2027. The referendum was subsequently scheduled for 29 August 2026. The question asks whether Iceland should resume accession negotiations with the EU — not whether to join. This is the first Icelandic government since Jóhanna Sigurðardóttir's government (2009–2013) to actively pursue EU-related policy. Prime Minister Kristrún Frostadóttir (Samfylkingin) and Foreign Minister Þorgerður Katrín Gunnarsdóttir (Viðreisn) have been the primary government figures advancing the referendum. Previous governments, including those led by Sigmundur Davíð Gunnlaugsson (2013–2016) and Katrín Jakobsdóttir (2017–2024), did not pursue EU accession despite intermittent public calls for a referendum.

Foreign Minister Þorgerður Katrín Gunnarsdóttir presented a parliamentary resolution (stjórnartillaga til þingsályktunar) on 9 March 2026 proposing a referendum on 29 August 2026 with the question: 'Á að halda áfram viðræðum um aðild Íslands að Evrópusambandinu?' (Should negotiations on Iceland's EU accession continue?). In her flutningsræða (sponsor's speech), she framed the referendum as a sovereignty issue — arguing that allowing the nation to decide is itself an exercise of sovereignty. She drew parallels with three historical decisions (NATO membership, EFTA accession, EEA agreement) that were also contentious at the time but are now widely regarded as beneficial. She stated that Iceland's 2009 EU application remains valid, confirmed by the EU itself, and that negotiations could resume 'around the turn of the year' if the public votes yes. She explicitly pledged: 'Ég mun aldrei skrifa undir samning sem tryggir ekki okkar markmið og okkar yfirráð' (I will never sign a deal that does not secure our goals and our sovereignty) — specifically regarding fisheries. She cited precedents for special arrangements: Nordic agricultural exemptions (Sweden, Finland) and property purchase restrictions (Denmark, Malta).

Iceland's total marine catch in 2024 was approximately 1.1 million tonnes, with the largest species by volume being capelin (c. 390,000 t), cod (c. 210,000 t), blue whiting (c. 130,000 t), and haddock (c. 55,000 t). Cod remains the most valuable species by landed value despite not being the highest by volume.

citations.report_title: 25. þáttur Silfursins: Stefnt að kosningu um ESB í sumar

claims.claim_text: Ríkisstjórnin hefur verið í baktjaldamakki í Brussel frá 2024 um ESB-aðild.

Þingsályktunartillaga ríkisstjórnarinnar snýst um fullt forræði yfir auðlindum þjóðarinnar, en í tillögunni er talað um "forræði" en ekki "fullt forræði". Forsætisráðherra var ekki tilbúinn í dag að lýsa því yfir að fullt forræði væri skilyrði.
Kristrún Frostadóttir sagði í aðdraganda kosninga 2024 að hún ætlaði ekki í ESB-vegferðina vegna brýnna verkefna heima fyrir, og endurtók það í áramótaávarpi sínu.
Kristrún Frostadóttir mætti ekki í Silfurbergið til að ræða ESB-málið og tók ekki þátt í þingumræðunni; hún gekk út úr þingsalnum um leið og umræður hófust.
Ríkisstjórnin setti ESB-málið á oddinn vegna þess að hún var komin í þrot með efnahagsmálin sem hún var kosin út á — verðbólgu, vexti og stöðu heimilanna.
Marta Kos, stækkunarstjóri ESB, hefur ítrekað sagt að samið sé á grundvelli þess umhverfis og raunveruleika sem hvert aðildarríki sem sækir um býr við.
Forsætisráðherra sagði á blaðamannafundi á föstudagi og ítrekaði í þinginu í dag að þjóðin þurfi ekki að verða sérfræðingar í ESB til að kjósa 29. ágúst.
ESB-þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslan stóð í stjórnarsáttmálanum og var kynnt á fyrsta fundi ríkisstjórnarinnar.
